mnemonics.title: DESIGN Checklist
mnemonics.content:
D - Define Design Tokens
E - Establish Color System
S - Set Typography Scale
I - Implement Component Library
G - Create Grid System
N - Add Navigation Patterns

Helps remember key elements of detailed design phase.

mnemonics.title: VISUAL Hierarchy
mnemonics.content:
V - Visual Weight (size, contrast)
I - Isolation (whitespace)
S - Sequence (reading pattern)
U - Unity (grouping)
A - Alignment (grid)
L - Layering (z-index)

Remember visual hierarchy principles.

acronyms.usage: International standards for making web content accessible to people with disabilities, defines contrast ratios, keyboard navigation, and screen reader support.
The visual elements and interactive components users see and interact with in a digital product.
The overall experience users have while interacting with a product, including usability, accessibility, and satisfaction.
Four principles of visual design: Contrast for emphasis, Repetition for consistency, Alignment for organization, Proximity for grouping.

acronyms.content: A methodology for creating design systems: Atoms → Molecules → Organisms → Templates → Pages

visualTips.visualization: Draw a color wheel and mark primary colors at top, complementary opposite, analogous as neighbors. Create a mood board with 3-5 colors max.
Use a modular scale (1.2, 1.25, 1.414 ratio). Draw hierarchy pyramid with largest heading at top, body text at base. Each level 20-30% smaller.
Create a spacing scale (4, 8, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64px). Visualize as nested boxes showing how spacing compounds.
Draw 12-column grid as vertical lines. Show how content spans different column counts (full=12, half=6, third=4). Mark gutters clearly.
Create a state matrix: rows for components, columns for states (default, hover, active, disabled, focus). Show visual changes in each cell.

analogies.analogy: Like a recipe book - ingredients (tokens) + cooking instructions (guidelines) = consistent dishes (UI) every time.
Like DNA - fundamental building blocks that define visual characteristics, passed through all design layers identically.
Like newspaper layout - biggest headline catches eye first, subheadings next, body text last - in deliberate order.
Like furniture arrangement - not crowding pieces together lets you move comfortably and see relationships clearly.
Like body language - small expressions and gestures that communicate feedback without words.
Like water - taking shape of container while staying the same substance, adapting to any screen size.
